parsed.message:
Your geyser appears to have a corroded internal tank, which is causing the rusty brown water in the drip tray and the leak you are observing. This internal corrosion is also likely responsible for the reduced hot water duration and the increase in your electricity bill.

This issue typically develops when the sacrificial anode inside the geyser tank has been fully consumed. The anode is designed to corrode instead of the steel tank, but once it is gone, the tank's steel walls begin to rust through, leading to leaks and discoloured water.

If left unaddressed, the corrosion will worsen, leading to a larger leak or a catastrophic burst of the geyser tank. This could cause significant water damage to your property, including ceilings, walls, and flooring.

There is a risk of electrical shorting if water comes into contact with the geyser's electrical components. It is important to switch off the geyser's power at your distribution board to prevent this hazard.
image_descriptions: This image shows a black plastic drip tray beneath a geyser, filled with rusty brown water, indicating an active leak. A copper pipe connection above the tray has visible water droplets, confirming the source of the leak.

This close-up image focuses on the copper pipework and connections, revealing corrosion and mineral deposits around the joints. The background shows a wall with some water staining, suggesting prolonged leakage.
This image provides an exterior view of a residential building with open windows and a brick wall, offering context for the property but no direct evidence of the geyser fault itself.

contractor_checklist: Isolate the electrical supply to the geyser and shut off the main water supply.
Drain the existing geyser and safely remove the corroded unit from its location.
Install a new geyser of appropriate size and specifications, ensuring all plumbing and electrical connections are secure.
Issue a new Certificate of Compliance for the electrical work and ensure proper waste disposal of the old unit.
parsed.homeowner_prep: Switch off the geyser's circuit breaker at your home's main distribution board immediately to prevent electrical hazards and further damage.
parsed.diy_verification: Observe the colour of the hot water from your taps; if it is also rusty or discoloured, it further confirms internal tank corrosion.

Visible rusty water in drip tray in Image 1
Visible leak from pipe connection in Image 1
parsed.cascading_damage: Water damage to surrounding area, increased electricity consumption

critique.notes_for_human_review: The diagnosis is accurate and well-supported by both the user's description and the visual evidence. The agent correctly identified the core issue and provided a comprehensive explanation. The confidence level is appropriate for the clear signals present.

parsed.message:
Your garage door appears to have a missing or broken spring on one side, which is causing the door to hang unevenly and preventing it from opening correctly. The motor is likely struggling to lift the unbalanced weight of the door.

Garage door springs, whether torsion or tension type, are crucial for counterbalancing the door's weight. They are designed for a finite number of cycles and eventually fatigue and break, often with a loud noise. When one spring fails, the entire load shifts to the remaining spring and the opener motor, which are not designed to handle the full weight alone.
image_descriptions: This image shows the lower left side of the garage door, revealing the lifting arm and its attachment point to the door frame. There is no visible spring mechanism present on this side, which is a key observation.

The full interior view of the garage door is visible here, clearly showing it hanging at an angle, lower on the left side. The ceiling-mounted opener motor and its rail are also visible, with the emergency release cord hanging down.

This close-up shot focuses on the central connection point between the garage door opener's rail and the top of the wooden door. The connecting arm and bracket appear to be intact and properly secured in this view.

A detailed view of the connecting bracket and pin on the top centre of the garage door is shown here. The pin securing the opener arm to the door is clearly visible and appears to be in place, confirming the connection is not detached at this point.

This close-up shot focuses on the central connection point between the garage door opener's rail and the top of the wooden door. The connecting arm and bracket appear to be intact and properly secured in this view.

A detailed view of the connecting bracket and pin on the top centre of the garage door is shown here. The pin securing the opener arm to the door is clearly visible and appears to be in place, confirming the connection is not detached at this point.
contractor_checklist: Inspect the garage door system to identify the exact type of spring (torsion or tension) and confirm the extent of the damage.
Replace the broken or missing spring with a new one appropriate for the door's weight and size.
Rebalance the garage door to ensure smooth and even operation.
Adjust the opener motor's limits and force settings after the spring replacement to prevent future strain.
parsed.homeowner_prep: Ensure the garage door is disengaged from the motor using the emergency release cord and do not attempt to operate it manually.
parsed.diy_verification: With the motor disengaged, attempt to lift the door manually; if it feels extremely heavy on one side, it confirms a spring issue on that side.
confidence_drivers: User explicitly stated 'spring is missing on one side'
Garage door visibly sagging on one side in wide shot
No spring mechanism visible on the left side in close-up
Symptoms (partial opening, motor beeping) consistent with unbalanced door

There was no conflicting evidence, and the diagnosis directly addressed the user's description and the visible symptoms.

parsed.message:
Your geyser is experiencing internal tank corrosion, which is why the water collecting in the drip tray has a distinct rusty brown colour. When the internal sacrificial anode is fully consumed, the water begins to attack the steel cylinder itself, leading to micro-fractures and structural weeping. This rust and scale buildup also coats the heating element, forcing it to run longer to heat the water, which directly explains your increased electricity bills and why the hot water runs out so quickly.

This is a progressive failure that will eventually lead to a complete rupture of the inner cylinder. If left unattended, the slow leak can overwhelm the drip tray or cause the tank to burst entirely, leading to sudden and extensive water damage to your ceilings and floors below.
parsed.action_required: Your technician will isolate the water supply and electrical connection to the geyser. They will drain the remaining water from the cylinder, disconnect the plumbing fittings, and remove the failed unit. New geyser will be installed along with a new drip tray, and the system will be commissioned and certified.

An under-shelf view showing white flexible pipes and copper pipes passing through a wooden board, with visible dark water stains on the timber surface.
An exterior view of an apartment building walkway, showing the outer wall and window frames near where the geyser overflow pipes exit.

parsed.thought: The images show a wooden tilt-up garage door with a ceiling-mounted opener. Comparing the two sides in the full-width view, there is a heavy tension spring on the right-hand side jamb, but the left-hand side jamb has an empty bracket with no spring. This severe counterbalance imbalance causes the door to be extremely heavy on one side, which overloads the opener motor and triggers its safety reverse mechanism.

Comparing the two sides in the full-width view, there is a heavy tension spring on the right-hand side jamb, but the left-hand side jamb has an empty bracket with no spring. thoughts_history.thought: The images show a wooden tilt-up garage door with a ceiling-mounted opener. Comparing the two sides in the full-width view, there is a heavy tension spring on the right-hand side jamb, but the left-hand side jamb has an empty bracket with no spring. This severe counterbalance imbalance causes the door to be extremely heavy on one side, which overloads the opener motor and triggers its safety reverse mechanism.

parsed.message:
The garage door is failing to open because the tension spring on the left-hand side is missing, leaving only the right-hand spring to support the weight of the heavy wooden door. This creates a severe physical imbalance that forces the opener motor to lift far more weight than it is designed to handle, triggering its safety overload sensor to stop and reverse the door.

Operating a heavy wooden tilt-up door without both counterbalance springs intact places immense stress on the motor drive gear and the remaining spring. The remaining spring is now under double the normal operating load and is at high risk of snapping suddenly.
parsed.action_required: Your technician will inspect the garage door hardware and install a matching replacement tension spring on the left-hand side. They will then adjust the tension on both sides to ensure the door is perfectly balanced and operates smoothly. Finally, they will test the motor limits and safety reversal settings to confirm correct operation.
image_descriptions: A close-up of the left-hand side tilt mechanism showing the pivot arm and mounting bracket on the wall jamb, with no tension spring attached.
A wide view of the entire wooden garage door from the inside, showing a tension spring present on the right jamb but completely absent on the left jamb.
A view looking up at the ceiling-mounted opener rail and the red emergency release cord hanging down near the top of the door.
A close-up of the metal header bracket bolted to the top centre of the wooden door panel, showing the connection to the opener drawbar.

structured_clarification.intro: Two things could be causing this — answering one quick question will narrow it down.

hypotheses.label: Missing Tension Spring
hypotheses.why: The left-hand tension spring is completely absent from the jamb bracket, causing a severe weight imbalance.
hypotheses.discriminating_question: Is the left-hand side spring completely missing from the frame?

answer_chips.text: Yes, the left spring is missing.

contractor_checklist: Supply and install a matching replacement tension spring on the left-hand side jamb.
Balance the door tension on both sides so it can be lifted easily by hand.
Inspect the lifting arms, hinges, and bracket attachments for wear or deformation.
Test the opener motor force limits and safety reverse travel settings.
parsed.homeowner_prep: Disconnect the garage door opener from the power outlet to prevent anyone from accidentally operating the motor.
parsed.diy_verification: With the motor disengaged via the red cord, attempt to lift the door manually; if it feels extremely heavy and drops instantly when released, the counterbalance system is failed.

User description matches the missing spring exactly
Motor behaviour is consistent with an overloaded safety reverse
